easy.question: The Moon shines because it:
easy.explanation: The Moon does not emit its own light. It is visible because it reflects the sunlight that falls on it.

easy.question: The complete cycle from one full Moon to the next takes about ______ days.
easy.explanation: The full Moon cycle (from one full Moon to the next) takes approximately 29.5 days, which is nearly a month.

easy.question: The waning period (decreasing bright portion) of the Moon is called:
easy.explanation: The waning period from full Moon to new Moon is called Krishna Paksha. The waxing period is called Shukla Paksha.

easy.question: On a new Moon day (______), the illuminated side of the Moon faces away from Earth.
easy.explanation: On Amavasya (new Moon), the illuminated half of the Moon faces the Sun and away from Earth, so the Moon is not visible.

medium.question: Explain why the Moon appears to change shape over a month. Is the Moon actually changing shape?
medium.answer: The Moon is always a sphere and does not actually change shape. The Sun illuminates one half of the Moon at all times. As the Moon revolves around Earth, we see different portions of its illuminated side. This creates the phases: full Moon (we see the entire lit side), half Moon (we see half the lit side), crescent (we see a thin sliver), and new Moon (the lit side faces away from us). The phases are NOT caused by Earth's shadow -- that would be a lunar eclipse. They occur simply because of the changing angle between the Sun, Moon, and Earth.
medium.explanation: Moon phases result from our changing viewing angle of the always-half-illuminated Moon.

medium.question: Why do Indian festivals like Diwali and Holi fall on different dates in the Gregorian calendar each year?
medium.answer: Many Indian festivals are tied to specific phases of the Moon: Diwali is celebrated on the new Moon (Amavasya) of the month of Kartika, and Holi is celebrated on the full Moon (Purnima) of the month of Phalguna. Since the lunar month (29.5 days) is shorter than the Gregorian calendar month, the Moon phases shift relative to the Gregorian calendar. The lunar year (354 days) is about 11 days shorter than the solar year (365 days), so lunar dates drift each year in the Gregorian calendar.
medium.explanation: The difference between lunar and solar calendars causes Indian festival dates to shift in the Gregorian calendar.

hard.question: Describe the complete sequence of Moon phases from Purnima (full Moon) back to the next Purnima, including both the waning and waxing periods.
hard.answer: Starting from Purnima (full Moon), the cycle proceeds as follows: WANING PERIOD (Krishna Paksha) -- about 2 weeks: (1) Full Moon (Purnima) -- entire illuminated side visible. (2) Waning Gibbous -- more than half still visible, but decreasing. (3) Half Moon (Third Quarter) -- exactly half visible, about a week after full Moon. (4) Waning Crescent -- less than half visible, a thin sliver. (5) New Moon (Amavasya) -- Moon not visible as the illuminated side faces away from Earth. WAXING PERIOD (Shukla Paksha) -- about 2 weeks: (6) Waxing Crescent -- a thin sliver appears, growing. (7) Half Moon (First Quarter) -- exactly half visible, about a week after new Moon. (8) Waxing Gibbous -- more than half visible, continuing to grow. (9) Full Moon (Purnima) -- entire illuminated side visible again. The complete cycle takes about 29.5 days. A waning Moon is easiest to spot at sunrise, and a waxing Moon at sunset. On full Moon day, the Moon rises roughly when the Sun sets. The Moon rises about 50 minutes later each day.
hard.explanation: Understanding the complete Moon phase cycle helps explain calendars, tides, and the timing of festivals.

easy.question: The Gregorian calendar is a:
easy.explanation: The Gregorian calendar (used worldwide today) is a solar calendar designed to keep months synchronised with the seasons.

easy.question: The Indian National Calendar begins on ______ March each year.
easy.explanation: The Indian National Calendar is a solar calendar adopted in 1957, beginning on 22 March (the day after the spring equinox).

easy.question: A leap day is added every 4 years to account for:
options: The extra quarter day in Earth's revolution
easy.answer: The extra quarter day in Earth's revolution
easy.explanation: Earth takes 365 days and 6 hours to orbit the Sun. The extra 6 hours accumulate to one full day in 4 years, added as February 29.

easy.question: In luni-solar calendars, an extra month called ______ Maasa is added every 2-3 years.
easy.explanation: Adhika Maasa (intercalary month) is added to keep the luni-solar calendar in sync with the seasons.

medium.question: Compare lunar, solar, and luni-solar calendars.
medium.answer: Lunar calendar: Based on Moon phases. A month is about 29.5 days, giving a year of about 354 days (11 days shorter than solar year). Seasons drift over time. Solar calendar: Based on Earth's revolution around the Sun. A year is 365 days (plus a leap day every 4 years). Months stay synchronised with seasons. Example: Gregorian calendar. Luni-solar calendar: Uses Moon phases for months but adds adjustments (like the extra Adhika Maasa month every 2-3 years) to stay in sync with seasons. Many Indian calendars follow this system.
medium.explanation: Different calendar systems balance the need to track Moon phases and seasonal cycles differently.

medium.question: Name three types of satellites launched by ISRO and their purposes.
medium.answer: Three types: (1) Cartosat series -- captures high-quality images of Earth for mapping and monitoring. (2) AstroSat -- makes scientific observations of stars and celestial objects. (3) Chandrayaan missions -- designed to study the Moon, including its surface and mineral composition. Other notable missions include Aditya L1 (to study the Sun) and Mangalyaan (Mars Orbiter Mission).
medium.explanation: ISRO's diverse satellite programme serves communication, navigation, weather monitoring, and scientific research.

hard.question: Three natural periodic events helped ancient people create calendars: the day, the month, and the year. Explain the astronomical basis of each and how they are used in different calendar systems.
hard.answer: The three periodic events: (1) THE DAY: Caused by Earth's rotation on its axis. The Sun appears to rise and set every day. The time from one noon to the next (mean solar day) is 24 hours. All calendars use this as the basic unit of time. (2) THE MONTH: Based on the Moon's phase cycle. The Moon completes one full cycle of phases (new Moon to new Moon) in about 29.5 days. This natural period became the basis for a month. Lunar calendars directly use this period for their months. (3) THE YEAR: Based on Earth's revolution around the Sun, taking about 365.25 days. During this time, one complete cycle of seasons occurs. Solar calendars use this as their year. The challenge for calendar-makers has been reconciling these three cycles, since 12 lunar months (354 days) do not equal one solar year (365.25 days). Lunar calendars let seasons drift. Solar calendars (like the Gregorian) adjust month lengths to match the solar year and add leap days. Luni-solar calendars (like many Indian calendars) use lunar months but periodically add an extra month (Adhika Maasa) to catch up with the solar year. This is why festivals tied to Moon phases (like Diwali) shift dates in the Gregorian calendar.
hard.explanation: Understanding the astronomical basis of days, months, and years explains why different calendar systems exist.
